---
title: "DuckDB 1.5 with spatial updates"
author:
  - name: Ralph Straumann
    url: https://ralphstraumann.ch
date: "2026-03-22 18:35"
image: "alejandro-correa-bayardo-unsplash.jpg"
description: "DuckDB 1.5 arrives with significant updates for #geospatial: GEOMETRY becomes a built-in data type, storage switches to #WKB with shredding for better compression, #CRS awareness is now part of the type system, and more! Here is a rundown of the most relevant new features in #DuckDB 1.5."
---

A few days ago, [DuckDB 1.5.0 was released](https://duckdb.org/2026/03/09/announcing-duckdb-150)[^duckdb].
This release includes a large number of updates, some of which specifically for 
geospatial applications. For example:

## [GEOMETRY becomes a built-in data type](https://duckdb.org/2026/03/09/announcing-duckdb-150#geometry-rework)

This move mirrors the approach recently taken by Parquet.[^parquet] From the announcement:

> Geospatial data is no longer niche. (...) By moving GEOMETRY into DuckDB’s 
core, extensions can now produce and consume geometry values natively, without 
depending on spatial. While the spatial extension still provides most of the 
functions for working with geometries, the type itself becomes a shared 
foundation that the entire ecosystem can build on.

And:

> This change also enables deeper integration with DuckDB’s storage engine and 
query optimizer, unlocking new compression techniques, query optimizations, and 
CRS awareness capabilities that were not possible when GEOMETRY only existed as 
an extension type.

## [Improved storage as WKB](https://duckdb.org/2026/03/09/announcing-duckdb-150#improved-storage-wkb-and-shredding)

`GEOMETRY` values are now stored as Well-Known Binary. This replaces a custom
format that was used previously in the `spatial` extension.

> We’ve also implemented a new storage technique specialized for `GEOMETRY`. 
When a geometry column contains values that all share the same type and vertex 
dimensions, DuckDB can additionally apply "shredding": rather than storing 
opaque blobs, the column is decomposed into primitive `STRUCT`, `LIST`, and 
`DOUBLE` segments that compress far more efficiently. This can reduce on-disk 
size by roughly 3x for uniform geometry columns such as point clouds.

## [Geometry statistics and query optimization](https://duckdb.org/2026/03/09/announcing-duckdb-150#geometry-statistics-and-query-optimization)

Sounds like `GEOMETRY` columns now receive a kind of automatic spatial indexing 
treatment:

> `GEOMETRY` columns now track per-row-group statistics - including the 
bounding box and the set of geometry types and vertex dimensions present. The 
query optimizer can use these to skip row groups that cannot match a query's 
spatial predicates, similar to min/max pruning for numeric columns.

## [CRS awareness](https://duckdb.org/2026/03/09/announcing-duckdb-150#coordinate-reference-system-support)

The `GEOMETRY` type now has optional support for a CRS[^crs] parameter, making the
CRS explicit part of the data type system rather than a metadata component.
Spatial functions enforce CRS consistency across their inputs, elminating a
common source of errors in geospatial applications.

> Only a couple of CRSs are built in by default, but loading the spatial 
extension registers over 7,000 CRSs from the EPSG[^epsg] dataset.

Finally, besides the DuckDB core, the `SPATIAL` extension also received an 
[upgrade](https://duckdb.org/2026/03/09/announcing-duckdb-150#spatial-extension).

Very exciting developments!

[^duckdb]: [`DuckDB`](https://www.duckdb.org/why_duckdb) is an open-source column-oriented Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) designed for analytics use-cases.

[^parquet]: An open-source, columnar storage file format designed for efficient data processing.

[^crs]: Coordinate reference system.

[^epsg]: The [EPSG Geodetic Parameter Dataset](https://epsg.org/) is a public registry of coordinate reference systems. "EPSG" stands for "European Petroleum Survey Group", the body that originally created it. The dataset assigns numeric codes to CRSs (e.g., EPSG:4326 for WGS 1984).
